text: The surgeon inserts a single port through the umbilical skin incision and a 5mm conventional trocar in the right lower quadrant. The peritoneum at the retrocecal recess is incised, followed by inferior to superior mobilization of the cecum and ascending colon using monopolar cautery and ultrasonic shear.

The surgeon exposes an avascular surgical plane along Toldt's line, revealing the duodenum, pancreas, and right kidney. A lateral to medial dissection is performed, exposing the posterior side of the superior mesenteric vein. The dissection continues up to the subhepatic area, followed by hepatic mobilization.
The surgeon incises the visceral peritoneum anterior to the central vessels, performing primary vascular control and DIII lymphadenectomy along the superior mesenteric vessels.

The surgeon uses a multifunctional ultrasonic device for dissection, grasping, cauterization, sealing, and cutting of vessels. The lateral to medial approach allows for extensive lymph node dissection around the superior mesenteric vessels.

The surgeon divides and ligates the ileocolic artery at its root using a hemolog clip. Subsequently, the ileocolic vein, draining into the superior mesenteric vein, is ligated and transected at its root.

Following lymph node dissection around the middle colic vessels, the surgeon identifies the branches of the middle colic artery. Bleeding from the right branch of the middle colic artery is controlled using an ultrasonic device, which securely seals the vessel without the need for a surgical clip.

The final intraoperative view shows the complete mesocolic excision and central vascular ligation achieved through reduced port laparoscopic access for right-sided colon cancer. A side-to-side intracorporeal anastomosis is performed using an articulated laparoscopic stapler.
The surgeon closes the common entry hole using continuous knotless barbed sutures, ensuring secure closure of the surgical site.
